title-group.article-title: Scale-Up Isolation of High-Purity Shatavarins from Asparagus racemosus via Flash Chromatography with Bioavailability and Pharmacological Insights

contrib-group.aff: 77290Natural Remedies Pvt Ltd, Bengaluru, Karnataka 560 100, India

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Authors. Published by American Chemical Society

license.license-p: This article is licensed under CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0

abstract.p: Asparagus racemosus, commonly known as Shatavari, is an established Ayurvedic plant whose bioactivity is credited to its steroidal saponins, generally referred to as shatavarins, exerting adaptogenic and estrogenic effects. However, traditional methods of isolation often produce products with limited purity and poor reproducibility, which seriously limit scalability in pharmacological applications. This study describes an effective, scalable, flash chromatography-based approach toward the isolation and purification of six bioactive steroidal saponins, AR-1 to AR-6, from AR root material. The methanolic extract was subjected to successive liquid–liquid partitioning and multistep chromatographic purification to give high-purity compounds identified as spirostan derivatives (AR-1 to AR-4), shatavarins, and aglycone derivatives (AR-5 and AR-6). The structural identities and chemical homogeneity were clearly supported by various spectroscopic analyses: HPLC-ELSD, FTIR, NMR, and LC-MS. The in silico ADME predictions performed by SwissADME were used as a prioritization strategy to inform compound selection for biological testing, providing relative comparisons of absorption potential rather than validating oral bioavailability. Derivatives of aglycone compounds AR-5 and AR-6 showed greater predicted absorption potential, while highly glycosylated saponins AR-2 and AR-4 showed low permeability, as expected for compounds of their size and polarity. While steroidal saponins are not typical of oral drug chemical space, these models correlate well with permeability and metabolic properties. Molecular docking studies of GPER and estrogen receptors (ERα, ERβ, and ERRγ) showed binding affinities of −7.7 to −12.4 kcal/mol, with AR-1, AR-2, and AR-4 showing a preference for hydrophobic and hydrogen-bonding interactions in GPER and ERα binding pockets typical of estrogen ligands. The developed flash chromatography workflow represents an efficient, scalable route to pharmacologically relevant standards of shatavarins. Combined with chromatographic and in silico results, the findings reveal the translational potential of AR saponins as multitarget phytoestrogens for hormone-modulatory and adaptogenic therapeutic applications.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Asparagus racemosus (AR) is a perennial medicinal herb of the Asparagaceae family found in India and other Asian regions. Its tuberous roots are the major therapeutic part and are extensively utilized in Ayurveda, where it is classified as a Rasayana known to enhance vitality, immunity, and longevity. Ayurveda, including the Charaka Samhita and Astang Hridayam describes its traditional uses and support its safety and efficacy. ,

Modern phytopharmacological studies are collaborating with many of these traditional claims. Extracts of AR show strong antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, along with immune-modulatory, antiulcer, antidiarrheal, antidiabetic, antimicrobial, and neuroprotective activities. , Among its steroidal saponins, the shatavarin IV is regarded as a key active molecule and has shown activity against specific biological targets, such as Core 2 GlcNAc transferase inhibition. The justified link between its traditional applications and active chemical constituents highlights the need to isolate these molecules in pure form for mechanistic studies and quality control of herbal formulations.

Beyond its traditional uses, AR has become a commercially important plant due to its wide therapeutic spectrum and increasing market value. Shatavari-based formulations are commonly used for reproductive health, hormonal balance, lactation support, gastrointestinal protection, and stress management. Its adaptogenic and immune-supportive actions have expanded its use in nutraceuticals, functional foods, and herbal pharmaceuticals. Growing interest from the cosmeceutical sector has led to Shatavari extracts being utilized for antioxidant and antiaging applications. Large-scale cultivation and contract farming have intensified, increasing the need for authenticated raw material and rigorous quality control. Consequently, purified shatavarins are required as reference standards for analytical validation, bioassays, and regulatory compliance.

Plant and marine extracts contain hundreds of structurally diverse compounds with closely related polarity, making purification difficult. , Conventional approaches, such as maceration or percolation, are often slow, solvent-intensive, and yield low-resolution separations. Additionally, thermally sensitive constituents may degrade during harsh extraction conditions, complicating the representation of the true phytochemical profile. With a rising demand for high-purity natural products in research and commercial sectors, advanced chromatographic solutions have become essential. Steroidal saponins, such as shatavarins, pose unique purification challenges due to their amphiphilic structures. Their hydrophobic aglycone and hydrophilic sugar residues lead to complex behavior across stationary phases. , Many saponins lack strong chromophores, making routine UV detection insufficient; therefore, Integration of universal detectors, such as ELSD, allows efficient detection of saponins that lack UV chromophores, further supporting industrial-scale purification and standardization efforts. ,

Flash chromatography, a pressure-assisted improvement of preparative column chromatography, offers fast, efficient, and cost-effective separations. It enables larger sample loading, lower solvent consumption, better resolution, and limited compound degradation. Modern flash systems integrating ELSD overcome the major obstacle of detecting nonchromophoric saponins, allowing simultaneous monitoring of chromophoric and nonchromophoric analytes. Although widely applied in synthetic chemistry, its potential for the rapid isolation of complex natural product mixtures remains underexplored.

Despite the recognized pharmacological value of AR and its shatavarins, consistent large-scale isolation of highly pure compounds remains challenging. Traditional column chromatography often delivers variable yields and purities. The current work aims to develop and optimize a robust flash chromatographic strategy for isolating four major shatavarin saponins from AR roots. Emphasis is placed on achieving high purity, reproducibility, and suitability for downstream analytical and biological studies. Pure reference standards generated through this work will support the quality assurance of AR-based products and provide well-characterized molecules for mechanistic and pharmacological investigations. The in silico studies of bioavailability and molecular docking are performed for the isolated compounds. The method also demonstrates a practical and scalable purification platform for complex natural products, aligning with contemporary needs in separation science and herbal drug standardization. Overall, this study represents a significant advancement in the separation and standardization of bioactive saponins, aligning with the current scientific and industrial demand for high-purity natural compounds.

sec.title: Extraction and Isolation of AR-1 to AR-6 from Asparagus racemosus
sec.p: The powdered AR roots were extracted with methanol, yielding a 5 kg (10% w/w) extract. This extract was subjected to liquid–liquid partition, showing significant enrichment of saponins in the butanol fraction of 3300 g (69% w/w). Subsequent column chromatography (C1) of the butanol fraction with a mobile phase of PE:EA:M yielded 9 fractions, of which C1–F6 (75% EA/M, 1100 g) enriched with steroidal saponins, was further purified using column 2 (C2). The mobile phase used for C2 was CHCl3:M:W and 11 fractions were collected with different polarities of solvents, of which 3 fractions were enriched with compounds. Fraction C2–F3 (8:1:0.1, 215 g) on crystallization, yielded AR-5 (0.25 g), and fraction C2–F4 (7:1:0.1, 128 g) on acid hydrolysis yielded AR-6 (0.43 g). The enriched steroidal saponin fraction C2–F2 (9:1:0.1, 135 g) was further fractionated using Diaion HP-20 (C3) with a mobile phase of water and methanol, and 10 fractions were collected, out of which 2 fractions, C3–F9 (85% M/W, 68.2 g) and C3–F10 (100% M, 8.6 g), were taken for flash chromatography (Figure ).

sec.title: Scale-Up Technology by Flash Chromatography
sec.p: Final purification was conducted using flash chromatography (FC). For FC-01, 68.2 g of the C3–F9 mixture yielded AR-1 (2.06 g, white powder) at 50–65 min and 55% M/W mobile phase, and AR-2 (0.2 g, white crystalline powder) at 70–75 min and 70% M/W mobile phase. For FC-02, 8.6 g of the C3–F10 yielded AR-3 (0.115 g, off-white amorphous powder) at 55–63 min and 62% ACN/W mobile phase, and AR-4 (0.015 g, white powder) at 67–77 min and 78%ACN/W mobile phase (Figure ).

sec.title: Identification and Characterization of AR-1 to AR-6
AR-1, Asparanin B (Shatavarin IV)
sec.p: White crystalline powder; Rt: 9.945 min; FTIR (KBr, cm–1): 3425, 2926, 2359, 1447; 1H & 13C NMR (Pyridine, 400 MHz) resonances at δH 0.83 (s, 18-H3), 0.97 (s, 19-H3), 1.09 (d, 27-H3), 1.09 (d, 12a-H), 1.16 (m, 7a-H), 1.17 (d, 21-H3), 1.68 (d, 6‴-H), 1.77 (m, 12b-H), 1.80 (m, 7b-H), 4.70 (d, 2‴-H), 4.88 (d, 1′-H), 4.99 (dq, 5‴-H), 5.48 (d, 1″-H) and 5.93 (s, 1‴-H). δC 16.18 (C-21), 17.55 (C-27), 17.86 (C-18), 19.77 (C-6‴), 22.42 (C-11), 25.26 (C-19), 27.47 (C-24), 27.68 (C-2), 28.04 (C-6), 28.05 (C-7), 28.25 (C-1), 28.82 (C-25), 31.96 (C-4), 32.13 (C-23), 33.43 (C-15), 36.52 (C-8), 36.79 (C-10), 38.01 (C-5), 41.49 (C-9), 41.58 (C-12), 42.15 (C-13), 43.75 (C-20), 57.73 (C-14), 62.56 (C-6′), 64.20 (C-17), 64.24 (C-6″), 66.38 (C-26), 71.51 (C-5‴), 73.12 (C-4″), 73.82 (C-2‴), 74.06 (C-3‴), 75.28 (C-4‴), 76.60 (C-3), 77.71 (C-3′), 77.71 (C-2″), 78.34 (C-5′), 78.61 (C-4′), 79.22 (C-3″), 79.81 (C-5″), 82.61 (C-16), 84.08 (C-2′), 103.12 (C-1′), 103.70 (C-1‴), 106.91 (C-1″) and 110.97 (C-22); LC-MS: 909.78 (M+Na), 887 (M+), 741.67m/z, 417.46m/z; Molecular formula: C45H74O17. The 1H NMR spectrum showed several angular and side-chain methyl signals between δH values of 0.83–1.17 ppm, consistent with a furostanol aglycone backbone typical of shatavari saponins. Multiple downfield resonances at δH 4.70–5.93 ppm indicated the presence of several glycosidic protons, including anomeric signals corresponding to a multisugar chain. The 13C NMR spectrum supported this interpretation, with three anomeric carbons at δC 103.12, 103.70, and 106.91 ppm confirming a trisaccharide moiety linked to the aglycone. Additional oxygenated carbons in the δC 62–84 ppm region reflected the sugar ring carbons and glycosylation sites, while signals in the δC 16–36 ppm range matched the methyl, methylene, and methine carbons of the furostanol nucleus. The spectral pattern closely resembles shatavarin derivatives, which typically possess a furostanol aglycone with a trisaccharide chain attached at C-3. The chemical shifts around δC 76.60 (C-3) and δC 64.20–66.38 ppm for glycosylation sites support this structural arrangement. Collectively, these NMR data establish AR-1 as a typical shatavarin-type furostanol saponin featuring a trisugar chain (Figures , , , and Figure S1) NMR data were aligned with previously published literature by Jadhav and Bhutani.

caption.p: Chemical structures of compounds isolated from the dried roots of Asparagus racemosus: Asparanin B (AR-1), 25S-5β-spirostan-3β-yl-O-[O-α-L-rhamnopyranosyl (1→4)]-β-d-glucopyranoside (AR-2), Asparagoside A (AR-3), Asparanin A (AR-4), Diosgenin (AR-5), and Sarsasapogenin (AR-6).

HPLC-ELSD chromatograms of compounds isolated from the dried roots of Asparagus racemosus: (A) AR-1, (B) AR-2, (C) AR-3, (D) AR-4, (E) AR-5, and (F) AR-6. Separation was achieved on a Zorbax XDB C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm) using a mobile phase consisting of water, acetonitrile, and formic acid at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min.

LCMS chromatograms of compounds isolated from Asparagus racemosus: (A) AR-1, (B) AR-2, (C) AR-3, (D) AR-4, (E) AR-5, and (F) AR-6. Analysis was performed using a Shimadzu system coupled to a SCIEX QTRAP 4500 mass spectrometer in positive/negative ESI mode using a C18 Phenomenex column. Mobile phases consisted of (A) 0.1% formic acid in water and (B) 0.1% formic acid in acetonitrile at a flow rate of 0.4 mL/min.

sec.title: AR-2, 25S-5β-Spirostan-3β-yl-O-[O-α-L-rhamnopyranosyl (1→4)]-β-D-glucopyranoside
sec.p: White powder; Rt:13.37 min; FTIR (KBr, cm–1): 3382, 2926, 2360, 1448; 1H & 13C NMR (Pyridine, 400 MHz) resonances at δH 4.69 (s, 1″-H), 4.17 (d, 16-H), 4.13 (d, 1′-H), 4.00 (s, 3-H), 3.62 (s, 26a-H), 1.67 (d, 12-H), 1.30 (m, 14-H), 1.27 (m, 12b-H), 1.13 (d, 27-H), 1.05 (d, 21-H) and 0.80 (d, 19-H). δC 14.66 (C-21), 16.05 (C-27), 16.36 (C-18), 18.30 (C-6″), 20.93 (C-11), 23.61 (C-19), 25.96 (C-24), 26.16 (C-23), 26.51 (C-6), 26.99 (C-7), 26.75 (C-25), 29.73 (C-1), 30.24 (C-4), 30.68 (C-15), 31.91 (C-2), 34.99 (C-8), 35.30 (C-10), 36.71 (C-5), 40.00 (C-12), 40.07 (C-13), 40.65 (C-9), 42.25 (C-20), 56.23 (C-14), 61.32 (C-6′), 62.71 (C-17), 64.87 (C-26), 70.10 (C-5″), 72.42 (C-2″), 72.60 (C-3″), 73.80 (C-4″), 74.26 (C-2′), 75.33 (C-3), 76.56 (C-5′), 76.93 (C-3′), 78.07 (C-4′), 81.10 (C-16), 102.44 (C-1′), 102.74 (C-1″) and 109.48 (C-22); LC-MS: 725 (M+), 577.4 m/z, 417.29 m/z. The exhibited NMR features are consistent with a furostanol-type steroidal saponin typical of AR. The 1H NMR spectrum showed characteristic methyl doublets at δH 1.13 (27-H), 1.05 (21-H), and 0.80 (19-H), along with multiple methine and methylene signals between δH 1.27–1.67 ppm, supporting a furostanol aglycone backbone. The presence of downfield resonances at δH 4.69 (1″-H), 4.17 (16-H), and 4.13 (1′-H) indicated glycosylation, with signals corresponding to anomeric and oxygenated protons typical of sugar attachments at C-3 and C-26. The 13C NMR spectrum further confirmed this assignment, displaying two anomeric carbons at δC 102.44 and 102 ppm, consistent with a disaccharide moiety. Several oxygenated carbons in the δC 61–81 ppm region supported the presence of glycosidic sugar units, while the remaining aliphatic carbon signals between δC 14–42 ppm corresponded to the furostanol nucleus. Altogether, these NMR features establish AR-2 as a furostanol saponin bearing a disugar chain, structurally consistent with known shatavarin-type metabolites from AR (Figures , , and Figure S2) NMR data was aligned with previously published literature by Jadhavand Bhutani.

sec.title: AR-3, Asparagoside A
sec.p: Off-white to white amorphous powder; Rt:14.818 min; FTIR (KBr, cm–1): 3380, 2927, 2360, 1448; 1H & 13C NMR (Pyridine, 400 MHz) resonances at δH 4.84 (d, 1′-H), 4.01 (m, 26a-H), 3.42 (d, 26b-H), 2.57 (d, 21-H), 2.31 (d, 27-H), 2.01 (s, 19-H3) and 1.90 (s, 18-H3). δC 16.17 (C-21), 17.55 (C-27), 17.87 (C-18), 22.43 (C-11), 25.13 (C-19), 27.30 (C-24), 27.47 (C-23), 27.67 (C-6), 28.02 (C-7), 28.28 (C-2), 28.81 (C-25), 31.72 (C-4), 32.24 (C-1), 33.42 (C-15), 36.50 (C-10), 36.81 (C-8), 38.24 (C-5), 41.50 (C-9), 41.57 (C-12), 42.16 (C-13), 43.76 (C-20), 57.73 (C-14), 64.18 (C-6′), 64.21 (C-17), 66.37 (C-26), 73.09 (C-4′), 75.56 (C-3), 76.67 (C-2′), 79.70 (C-3′), 80.02 (C-5′), 82.60 (C-16), 104.35 (C-1′) and 110.98 (C-22); LC-MS: 579 (M+H), 417.32 m/z, 399.195m/z; molecular formula: C33H54O8. The exhibited NMR characteristics are consistent with a monoglycosylated furostanol saponin. The 1H NMR spectrum showed angular methyl singlets at δH 2.01 and 1.90 ppm and two methyl doublets at δH 2.57 and 2.31 ppm, confirming a typical furostanol aglycone core. The downfield proton at δH 4.84 (1′-H) indicated an anomeric sugar proton, while the resonances at δH 4.01 and 3.42 (26-H) reflected oxygenation at C-26. The 13C NMR spectrum supported these findings, showing a distinct anomeric carbon at δC 104.35 ppm and additional oxygenated carbons at δC 66.37 (C-26), 75.56 (C-3), and 82.60 (C-16). Aliphatic carbons resonating between δC values of 16–43 ppm confirmed the furostanol nucleus. The data establish AR-3 as a monoglycosylated furostanol saponin compatible with shatavarin-type metabolites (Figures , , , and Figure S3). NMR data was aligned with previously published literature by Huang XF et al.

sec.title: AR-4, Asparanin A
sec.p: White amorphous powder; Rt: 2.110 min; FTIR (KBr, cm–1): 3458, 2923, 2360, 1586; LC-MS: 739 (M-H), 577.309 m/z, 160.87 m/z; molecular formula: C39H64O13 (Figures , , , and Figure S4)

sec.title: AR-5, Diosgenin
sec.p: White crystalline powder; Rt:20.4 min; FTIR (KBr, cm–1): 3436, 3290, 2927, 2848, 2363; 1H & 13C NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz) resonances at δH 0.79 (s, 18-H), 0.78 (t, 27-H), 0.79 (s, 21-H), 1.02 (s, 19-H), 3.34 (t, 26a-H), 3.45 (dd, 26b-H), 3.53 (br s, 3a-H), 4.38 (q, 16-H), and 5.34 (br d, 6-H). δC 140.81 (C-5), 121.42 (C-6), 109.29 (C-22), 80.83 (C-16), 71.74 (C-3), 66.85 (C-26), 62.12 (C-17), 56.53 (C-14), 50.08 (C-9), 42.29 (C-4), 41.62 (C-20), 40.27 (C-13), 39.80 (C-12), 37.23 (C-1), 36.65 (C-10), 32.06 (C-7), 31.85 (C-15), 31.63 (C-2), 31.45 (C-8), 31.40 (C-23), 30.30 (C-25), 28.81 (C-24), 20.88 (C-11), 19.41 (C-19), 17.12 (C-27), 16.52 (C-18), and 14.74 (C-21). LC-MS: 415.65 (M+H), 397.3, 271.14, 253.19, 137.09 m/z; molecular formula: C27H42O3, displayed 1H and 13C NMR characteristics consistent with a steroidal sapogenin. The 1H NMR spectrum showed four angular methyl groups at δH 0.78–1.02 ppm and an olefinic proton at δH 5.34 ppm, indicating unsaturation in the steroid nucleus. Signals at δH 3.34 and 3.45 ppm corresponding to the 26-H protons, together with δH 4.38 (16-H), reflected oxygenation at C-16 and C-26. The 13C NMR spectrum confirmed these assignments with downfield, oxygen-bearing carbons at δC 80.83 ppm (C-16), 71.74 ppm (C-3), and 66.85 ppm (C-26). The olefinic carbons δC 140.81 ppm (C-5) and 121.42 ppm (C-6) supported the presence of unsaturation. The NMR data demonstrate that AR-5 is a hydroxylated steroidal sapogenin consistent with shatavarin-derived aglycones (Figures , , , and Figure S5) NMR data was aligned with previously published literature by Puri R et al. and Pazhanichamy K et al. ,

sec.title: AR-6, Sarsasapogenin
sec.p: White powder; Rt:2.143 min; FTIR (KBr, cm–1): 3502, 3317, 2928, 2860, 2345; 1H & 13C NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz) resonances at δH 4.37 (q, 17-H), 4.10 (br s, 3-H), 3.93 (dd, 26-H), 3.28 (d, 26-H), 1.07 (d, 21-H), 0.97 (d, 27-H), 0.95 (s, 19-H), and 0.88 (s, 18-H). δC 14.30 (C-21), 16.49 (C-27), 16.70 (C-18), 20.90 (C-11), 23.91 (C-19), 25.78 (C-24), 25.96 (C-23), 26.54 (C-7), 26.56 (C-6), 27.09 (C-25), 27.82 (C-2), 29.95 (C-1), 31.75 (C-15), 33.55 (C-4), 34.99 (C-8), 35.28 (C-10), 36.53 (C-5), 39.87 (C-9), 40.32 (C-12), 40.68 (C-13), 42.13 (C-20), 56.48 (C-14), 62.12 (C-17), 65.14 (C-26), 67.10 (C-3), 81.02 (C-16), and 109.73 (C-22). LC-MS: 417.103­(M+H), 399.358, 273.132, 255.299, 137.07 m/z; molecular formula: C27H44O3 exhibited NMR features characteristic of a hydroxylated steroidal sapogenin. The 1H NMR spectrum showed four angular methyl signals at δH 1.07, 0.97, 0.95, and 0.88 ppm, confirming a typical steroidal framework. Downfield resonances at δH 4.37 (17-H), 4.10 (3-H) ppm, and δH 3.93/3.28 ppm (26-H) indicated hydroxylation at C-3, C-17, and C-26. The 13C NMR spectrum supported these assignments with corresponding oxygenated carbons at δC 67.10 ppm (C-3), 62.12 ppm (C-17), 65.14 ppm (C-26), and 81.02 ppm (C-16). The remaining aliphatic carbon signals δC 14–42 ppm were consistent with a saturated steroid nucleus, while δC 109.73 ppm (C-22) reflected typical furostanol-related carbon environments. The NMR data confirm that AR-6 is a polyhydroxylated steroidal sapogenin structurally aligned with the shatavarin-derived aglycone component (Figures , , , and Figure S6) NMR data was aligned with previously published literature by Agrawal PK et.al.

sec.title: Bioavailability Studies of Isolated Compounds from the Roots of Asparagus racemosus
sec.p: The SwissADME in silico ADME predictions allowed for comparative analysis as a prioritization strategy without validating oral bioavailability. Of the steroidal saponins tested, AR-5 and AR-6 had the most favorable predicted profiles, with Abbott bioavailability scores of 0.55, reflecting moderate systemic exposure. This is consistent with their high predicted GI absorption, moderate topological polar surface area (TPSA = 38.69 Å2), and low hydrogen bond donor/acceptor counts. In contrast, the highly glycosylated AR-2 and AR-4 had low scores (0.17), consistent with low GI absorption, reflecting a large molecular weight, high hydrogen bonding capacity, and very high TPSA values (≥167 Å2) that strongly impede passive transcellular diffusion. AR-3 was intermediate; despite high molecular weight and some drug-like property violations, its TPSA (117.84 Å2) and hydrogen bonding properties were within the upper limits of absorption, predicting high GI absorption similar to the aglycones.

These observations were supported by drug-like filter property compliance. AR-5 and AR-6 had generally favorable profiles according to Lipinski, Veber, Egan, and Muegge filters, with only single violations for high lipophilicity that could be optimized by formulation strategies. AR-2 and AR-4 had multiple violations according to Lipinski, Ghose, Veber, Egan, and Muegge filters, primarily reflecting excessive size, polarity, and rotatable bonds, predicting poor oral permeability. AR-3 had fewer and less severe violations, consistent with its improved absorption profile. Although steroidal saponins tend to be nonstandard oral drugs chemically because of their amphiphilic character and glycosylation patterns, these rule-based models are still useful for comparative analysis of relative permeability and metabolic stability trends among structurally similar scaffolds to inform development strategies. (Figure and Table ).

caption.p: Pharmacological activity of isolated compounds: AR-1 (refs. , ), AR-2 (ref. ), AR-4 (ref. ), AR-5 (ref. ), and AR-6 (ref. ) from the dried roots of Asparagus racemosus.

caption.title: Bioavailability Studies of Isolated Compounds AR-2 to AR-6

tr.td: No; 5 violations: MW > 600, TPSA > 150, #rings >7, H-acc >10, H-don >5
No; 5 violations: MW > 600, TPSA > 150, #rings >7, H-acc >10, H-don >5

sec.title: Molecular Docking Insights for AR-1 to AR-6
sec.p: In a series of docking analyses, six steroidal saponins were isolated from shatavari: AR-1, AR-2, AR-3, AR-4, AR-5, and AR-6, and were examined against the GPER as well as the estrogen receptors ERα, ERβ, and ERγ (ERRγ-like). These analyses revealed binding score values ranging between −4.7 and −12.4 kcal/mol, with a cavity volume of 405–8690 m3 and a grid size commensurate with the size of each individual ligand ranging between 23 and 35 mÅ. Each of the individual ligands bound its unique pocket (C1–C4) and displayed hydrophobic, π–π, hydrogen bonding, as well as electrostatic interactions between its receptor residues that align with known estrogen pharmacophores.

The highest ligand binding to the GPER C1 pocket (−9, 4, -16 Å, 8690 Å3) had scores of −9.1, −9.4, and −10.1 kcal/mol for compounds AR-1, AR-2, and AR-4, respectively. These ligands interacted with residues around an essential transmembrane fragment (GLU51, HIS52, GLN54, TYR55, ILE57, GLY58, LEU59, GLU115, ASN118, LEU119, HIS120, GLU121, ARG122, TYR124, and ASP125) and subpocket residues CYS205, PHE306/278, HIS282/300, PHE398, and MET309. The aromatic moieties assisted in nonspecifically stabilizing the steroid ring, while polar atoms contacted glycosidic oxygen atoms. The GPER C2 pocket (−6, 18, 16 Å, 780 Å3) ligands AR-3 and AR-6 interacted with residues LEU79, VAL82/83, PHE87, ARG253/322/326, and TRP331/334. Diosgenin showed shallow, weak engagement (−4.7 kcal/mol) with PHE146/153 and ILE181/229, underscoring glycosylation’s role in polar recognition.

In ERα, AR-4 had the highest binding affinity of −12.4 kcal/mol in the C1 pocket at position (6, −9, −4 Å; 1629 Å3) snuggled between LEU320/387/391/403/440, TRP393, and PHE445, with hydrogen bond contacts with GLU323/353/397 and ARG394/436. The remaining saponins, namely AR-1, AR-3, and AR-6, were docked at the common C2 pocket at position (−20, −4, 4 Å; 757 Å3) with binding affinities ranging from −8.3 to −9.0 kcal/mol, interacting with ARG300/363/477, LYS302/303/362, LEU306/308/310/370/372, and GLU22/470/471. AR-5 bound to a smaller C3 site (−8.3 kcal/mol), and AR-2 engaged a peripheral C4 pocket (−9.7 kcal/mol) with GLU385, TYR459, PHE461, and HIS513/516/547/550.

In ERβ, most of the ligands bound to the C2 pocket (−8, 22, −3 Å; 968 Å3) with affinities ranging from −7.7 to −8.5 kcal/mol through a series of interactions involving PRO253-ARG256, GLU257, LEU258–260/270/338/339, TRP312, TYR320, and LYS314/315/334. This was surrounded by a hydrophobic core rimmed by charged amino acids. AR-2 preferred the C1 site (−9.1 kcal/mol; 4, −2, 4 Å) involving VAL53–LEU79, TRP80, and LEU273/346/367/370.

The ERγ/ERRγ-like C3 pocket (−2, −1, 40 Å; 530 Å3) could bind all six saponins with binding constants ranging from −8.6 to −9.9 kcal/mol, while retaining the conserved interactions with TYR115/117/187, HIS116, LYS126/130/155/186/188, and ARG131/176/179/181/184. These residue interaction patterns position shatavari saponins as multitarget phytoestrogens that can elicit rapid GPER-mediated signaling and nuclear ER/ERR transcriptional modulation, in line with their potential in estrogen-responsive pathways (Tables S1 and S2).

sec.title: Plant Materials
sec.p: The roots of the plant AR were collected from Andhra Pradesh, India, and authenticated by FRLHT, Bengaluru (ref.No.FRLHT/RDR/Authen/2025/01/05). The roots were dried and powdered prior to extraction.

sec.title: Reagents and Instruments
sec.p: Solvents used for HPLC analysis were methanol of HPLC grade, obtained from Rankem (Avantor Performance Materials India Private Limited ). The solvents used for flash chromatography were methanol and acetonitrile of analytical grade and were purchased from Rankem (Avantor Performance Materials India Private Limited ). Solvents used for column chromatography were petroleum ether, ethyl acetate, and methanol. A rotary evaporator (Rotavapor RE11, Buchi, Switzerland), flash chromatography system (BUCHI Labortechnik AG, 9230 Flawil/Switzerland), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method utilizing an evaporative light scattering detector (ELSD), and 1H and 13C NMR were recorded on a Bruker Avance 400 MHz instrument. Pyridine was used as a solvent for NMR. FTIR spectra were recorded in KBr tablets using a Nicolet FTIR Avatar Nicolet (Thermo Scientific, USA) spectrometer in the wavelength range of 4000–400 cm‑1. LCMS was done using a SCIEX QTRAP 4500 instrument.

sec.title: Extraction and Isolation of Dried Roots of Asparagus racemosus
sec.p: To evaluate the solvents for extraction, we used 10 g of root powder and loaded it into a distillation chamber at 60–70 °C for three h with different solvents like ethyl acetate, methanol, and water, replicating the procedure three times each. The extracted solvents were collected, concentrated in a rotary evaporator, and dried. The dried extracts were dissolved in methanol and loaded onto HPLC.

The dried root powder of AR (50 kg) was extracted in a distillation unit apparatus using methanol (1:3) for 3 h maintained at a temperature of 70–85 °C. The filtrate was concentrated by a rotary evaporator (Rotavapor RE11, Buchi, Switzerland) and finally concentrated by a vacuum tray dryer to get a concentrated extract of 5 kg. Next, the extract undergoes partitioning, first being mixed with water (1:4), then with the nonpolar solvent ethyl acetate (1:3), followed by three washes and concentration, which yielded 250 g of fraction. After that, it was mixed with the midpolar solvent butanol (1:3), subjected to three washes, and concentrated again using a rotary evaporator to yield 3300 g of fraction. Finally, the remaining aqueous layer was concentrated and yielded 1250 g. The butanol partition extract was rich in quantity and chemical composition (Figure S8-A). N-butanol fractions were subjected to normal phase column (col-1) chromatography by using silica gel #60–120 mesh size. The mobile phase used was a gradient of 100% ethyl acetate to 100% methanol. 1100 g of 75% M/EA­(C1–F6) fractions, showing enrichment in the required compounds, was further proceeded with a second normal phase column (col-2) with silica #100–200 mesh size. The mobile phase was made up of chloroform (CHCl3), methanol (M), and water (W) (Figure S8–B). The fraction obtained from the mobile phase, 9:1:0.1­(C2–F2), was further proceeded for the Diaion column (col-3) with a mobile phase of water:methanol in a gradient phase (Figure S8–C). The enriched fraction obtained from col-3, 68.2 g of 85% M/W (C3–F9), and 8.6 g of 100% M (C3–F10) was subjected to flash chromatography (Figure S8-D). Fraction of 215 g from column 2, C2–F3 (mobile phase 8:3:0.1) on crystallization, yielded 0.25 g of AR-5. Another fraction of 128 g obtained from column 2, C2–F4 (mobile phase 7:3:0.1), was hydrolyzed by 5760 mL of 2 M hydrochloric acid for 3 h. After the solution cooled, the acid hydrolysis solution was neutralized with 4% sodium hydroxide. Then, the hydrolysis product was extracted with chloroform (3 × 3200 mL); the chloroform extracts were combined, and the solvent was removed by evaporation and concentrated to dryness at 60 °C to obtain 0.43 g of AR-6. Generally, AR-6 is present in the plant and its subsequent extract in its glycosidic form (ref. ). The identity of the isolated compounds was confirmed by comparing the spectroscopic data with the available literature.

sec.title: Method Development and Scale-Up by Flash Chromatography
sec.p: Further, the separation of compounds was carried out on an automated flash chromatography system. The instrument used was flash pure-BUCHI, C18 30 μm spherical columns (330 g); inner diameter 60 mm; length 270 mm; column volume 600 mL; pressure 180 psi; flow rate 100 mL/min. RP-Silica (particle size 35–60 μm) was purchased from BUCHI Labortechnik AG (9230 Flawil/Switzerland).Figure

caption.p: Systematic representation of the isolation scheme of saponins, glycosides, and aglycones AR-1 to AR-6 from the roots of Asparagus racemosus.

sec.p: 68.2 g of C3–F9 (68.2 g) was adsorbed with 205 g of RP silica and dried. It was packed in a cartridge and subjected to reverse-phase (C18) silica (FC-1) using a flash pure column, and given in 13 runs of 20 g each. The mobile phase composition was water:methanol (increasing proportion of methanol). Isolation of the compounds was carried out using step gradient analysis. The flow rate was 100 mL/min, the fraction collected, and volume was 20 mL, and the detection wavelengths were 200 and 205 nm. It yielded two compounds: the AR-1 peak was eluted at 50–65 min, 55% M/W, and collected from 10 to 25 number test tubes, and the AR-2 peak was eluted at 70–75 min, 70% M/W, and collected from 35 to 55 number test tubes. Test tube fractions were pooled together by TLC and dried. Finally, AR-1 yielded 2.06 g, and AR-2 yielded 0.2 g (Figure A, Table A).

caption.title: Gradient Tables of Flash Chromatography (A) FC-01 and (B) FC-02

caption.p: (A) A flash chromatography graph of isolated compounds, AR-1 eluting at 50–65 min and mobile phase of 55% M/W, AR-2 eluting at 70–75 min and mobile phase of 70% M/W, (B) AR-3 eluting at 55–63 min and mobile phase of 62% ACN/W, AR-4 eluting at 67–77 min and mobile phase of 78% ACN/W.

sec.p: 8.6 g of C3–F10 (8.6 g) was dissolved in methanol, adsorbed with 25.8 g of RP silica, and dried. It was packed in a cartridge and subjected to reverse-phase (C18) silica (FC-2) using a flash pure column and given in 3 runs of 10 g each. The mobile phase composition was water:acetonitrile (increasing proportion of acetonitrile). Isolation of the compounds was carried out using step gradient analysis. The flow rate was 100 mL/min, the fraction collected volume was 20 mL, and the detection wavelength was 200 and 205 nm. It yielded two compounds: the AR-3 peak was eluted at 55–63 min with 62% ACN/W and collected from 15 to 30 number test tubes, and the AR-4 peak was eluted at 67–77 min with 78% ACN/W and collected from 37 to 58 number test tubes. Test tube fractions were pooled together by TLC and dried. Finally, AR-3 yielded 0.115 g, and AR-4 yielded 0.015 g (Figure B, Table B). The isolated compounds were further taken for spectroscopic analysis.

sec.title: Analytical Fingerprinting of Isolated Compounds AR-1 to AR-6
sec.p: High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods utilizing an evaporative light scattering detector (ELSD) have been developed. The method uses a Zorbax XDB C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm) for separation, maintained at a temperature of 30 °C. The mobile phase consists of acetonitrile, Milli-Q-Water, and formic acid, all HPLC grade. A constant-flow HPLC pump delivers the mobile phase at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The injection volume is 5 μL, with a total run time of 20 min. The ELSD is set with an evaporator temperature of 60 °C and a nebulizer temperature of 60 °C to detect the separated compounds. The system used is an Agilent Infinity-II series module or its equivalent. Chromatographic separation was achieved using a Phenomenex Luna C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, 100 Å, 5 μm; Phenomenex, Torrance, CA, United States). The injection volume was 10 μL. Gradient elution was carried out with a mobile phase made up of solvent A (water containing 0.1% formic acid) and solvent B (0.1% formic acid in acetonitrile). The flow rate was set at 0.8 mL/min. Impurity peaks and HPLC duplicate runs of AR-1 to AR-6 are mentioned in Figure S7. Isolated compounds were submitted to FTIR, LCMS, and NMR analysis.

sec.title: Bioavailability Studies of Isolated Compounds from the Roots of Asparagus racemosus
sec.p: The physicochemical, pharmacokinetic, and drug-likeness properties of isolated shatavari saponins (AR-1 to AR-6) were predicted using the SwissADME web server http://www.swissadme.ch. The ligand structures were prepared in SMILES or SDF format from PubChem entries [AR-2, AR-3, AR-4, AR-5, AR-6], with AR-1 reconstructed for stereochemical accuracy.

Calculated molecular descriptors included molecular weight, logP (WLOGP, iLOGP, XLOGP3, and MLOGP), topological polar surface area, number of hydrogen bond donors/acceptors, rotatable bonds, and fractional polar surface area. Drug-likeness was assessed against five rule sets: Lipinski (Ro5), Ghose, Veber, Egan, and Muegge, with violations counted. Oral bioavailability was predicted using the Abbott Bioavailability Score (threshold 0.55 for high probability) and the BOILED-Egg model for gastrointestinal absorption and blood–brain barrier permeability.

Pharmacokinetic parameters included gastrointestinal absorption (high/low), P-glycoprotein substrate status, cytochrome P450 inhibition data, including CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4, and the predicted volume of distribution. Results were compiled into comparison tables (Table S1 ), highlighting structure-permeability relationships, with AR-5/AR-6 highlighted as ideal oral candidates: score 0.55, TPSA 38.69 Å2, high GI absorption, versus the very poorly permeable glycosides AR-2/AR-4: score 0.17, TPSA ≥ 167 Å2. All calculations utilized the default parameters of SwissADME without additional optimization.

sec.title: Molecular Docking Insights for AR-1 to AR-6
sec.p: Three-dimensional structures of GPER/GPR30, ERα, ERβ, and ERRγ were retrieved from the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (alphafold.ebi.ac.uk) as high-confidence predicted models (pLDDT > 90 across ligand-binding domains). Receptor preparation involved conversion to PDBQT format with standard protonation at physiological pH 7.4; missing regions were handled by AlphaFold’s intrinsic loop modeling without additional closure. Ligand structures for shatavari saponins AR-1, AR-3, AR-4, AR-5, and AR-6 were obtained from PubChem, while AR-2 was reconstructed from experimental NMR data using the ACD/NMR predictor.

Blind docking was performed via the CB-Dock2 web server (http://cadd.labshare.cn/site/docking), employing automated the Concavity analysis (Z-score > 1.0) for cavity detection rather than predefined active sites: GPER C1 (−9.4, −16.0, 16.0 Å; 8690 Å3), C2 (−6.0, 18.0, 16.0 Å; 780 Å3); ERα C1 (6.0, −9.0, −4.0 Å; 1629 Å3), C2 (−20.0, −4.0, 4.0 Å; 757 Å3), plus C3/C4; ERβ C1 (4.0, -2.0, 4.0 Å), C2 (−8.0, 22.0, −3.0 Å; 968 Å3); ERRγ C3 (−2.0, −1.0, 40.0 Å; 530 Å3). Docking grids were autogenerated (23–35 Å per axis; 0.375 Å spacing; exhaustiveness = 16) at cavity centroids. No redocking of known ligands (17β-estradiol, G-1) or repeated runs were performed for validation, and protonation states were not explicitly optimized beyond standard AutoDock tools.

Given the exploratory blind docking approach without positive controls, RMSD benchmarking, or pose reproducibility analysis, the resulting scores (−4.7 to −12.4 kcal/mol) and cavity-specific interaction patterns should be interpreted cautiously as hypothesis-generating rather than quantitative affinity rankings across targets/compounds.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study establishes a comprehensive, scalable, and analytically robust workflow for the isolation of steroidal saponins from AR using a combination of conventional partitioning, preliminary chromatographic enrichment, and high-resolution flash chromatography. The optimized flash chromatographic conditions enabled efficient separation of structurally similar saponins, yielding six major compounds, spirostan derivatives, namely Asparanin B (AR-1), 25S-5β-spirostan-3β-yl-O-[O-α-L-rhamnopyranosyl (1→4)]-β-d-glucopyranoside (AR-2), Asparagoside A (AR-3), Asparanin A (AR-4), Aglycone Diosgenin (AR-5), and Sarsasapogenin (AR-6) obtained through controlled acid hydrolysis. Flash chromatography proved to be a significantly more efficient and effective method compared to traditional column chromatography . It achieved a substantially higher product yield and purity while reducing the process time from days to mere hours. Furthermore, flash chromatography demonstrated superior sustainability and cost-effectiveness by requiring approximately 80% less solvent. Although column chromatography resulted in a higher total recovery rate, the lower purity suggests this recovery includes significant impurities. Therefore, flash chromatography is the preferred technique for isolating high-purity compounds rapidly (Figure ).

caption.p: Comparison between column chromatography and flash chromatography on yield, purity, solvent, recovery, and time for the isolation of Shatavarin IV.

sec.p: In silico ADME analysis identified AR-5 and AR-6 as having the most favorable predicted oral bioavailability profiles (Abbott score of 0.55), supported by high GI absorption predictions, moderate TPSA (38.69 Å2), low hydrogen-bond donors/acceptors, and minimal drug-likeness rule violations, primarily related to lipophilicity. AR-3 showed intermediate potential despite some violations, with TPSA (117.84 Å2) within absorption limits and high predicted GI uptake. Highly glycosylated AR-2/AR-4 exhibited poor profiles (score 0.17; TPSA ≥ 167 Å2) due to excessive molecular weight, polarity, rotatable bonds, and multiple Lipinski/Ghose/Veber/Egan/Muegge violations, suggesting aglycones and less polar glycosides as priority oral scaffolds.

Docking analyses indicate that Shatavari saponins AR-1 to AR-6 can plausibly engage GPER, ERα, ERβ, and ERRγ pockets with scores of −4.7 to −12.4 kcal/mol through hydrophobic, hydrogen-bonding, and electrostatic interactions resembling estrogen pharmacophores, with glycosylation appearing to enhance polar recognition versus aglycone controls. These computational results generate hypotheses of multitarget phytoestrogen potential, warranting experimental validation through ERα/ERβ reporter assays, GPER cAMP/calcium signaling readouts, and functional antagonism studies, rather than demonstrating confirmed receptor agonism, antagonism, or pathway modulation.

The successful scale-up of this methodology highlights its potential for routine production of high-quality reference standards, which are essential for phytochemical authentication, quantitative assay development, and regulatory compliance in the herbal and nutraceutical industries. Furthermore, the availability of purified shatavarins provides a valuable resource to advance pharmacological research on AR and to support mechanistic studies exploring its therapeutic relevance. Overall, this work delivers a practical and industry-relevant purification strategy that addresses existing challenges in saponin isolation and contributes to improved quality control and scientific exploration of this important medicinal plant.

ack.p: The authors acknowledge Mr. Swapnil Kamble and Mr. Anadhan K for their support in the authentication of raw materials and analysis for this project.

notes.p:
The Supporting Information is available free of charge at https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsomega.6c00145.
Spectral data of FTIR for the compounds AR-1 to AR-6; 1H NMR and 13C NMR for the compounds AR-1, AR-2, AR-3, AR-5, and AR-6; HPLC impurity peaks of isolated compounds AR-1 to AR-6; thin layer chromatography (TLC) of partition fractions, column-1, column-2, and column-3; molecular docking studies of isolated compounds AR-1 to AR-6; and molecular docking images of isolated compounds AR-1 to AR-6 (PDF)
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ref.mixed-citation:
Garde, G. K.
Marathia Translation of vagbhat’s Astangahridya; Aryabhushana Mudranalaya: Uttarstana, 1970; Vol.: 1970, pp. 40–48.

Frawley, D.
Ayurvedic healing: A comprehensive guide; Lotus Press, 2000.
